(frequently referred to as the **Oakland A's**) were an American [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB) team based in [Oakland, California](/wiki/Oakland,_California "Oakland, California"), from 1968 to 2024. The Athletics were a member club of the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") (AL) [West Division](/wiki/American_League_West "American League West") and played its home games at the [Oakland Coliseum](/wiki/Oakland_Coliseum "Oakland Coliseum") throughout their entire time in Oakland. The franchise's nine [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") championships, fifteen pennants, and seventeen division titles are the second-most in the AL after the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees").

The **Oakland Athletics** (frequently referred to as the **Oakland A's**) were an American [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB) team based in [Oakland, California](/wiki/Oakland,_California "Oakland, California"), from 1968 to 2024. The Athletics were a member club of the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") (AL) [West Division](/wiki/American_League_West "American League West") and played its home games at the [Oakland Coliseum](/wiki/Oakland_Coliseum "Oakland Coliseum") throughout their entire time in Oakland. The franchise's nine [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") championships, fifteen pennants, and seventeen division titles are the second-most in the AL after the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees").

The Athletics moved to Oakland from [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City_Athletics "Kansas City Athletics") in 1968, where the team had previously relocated in 1954 from its original home in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia_Athletics "Philadelphia Athletics"). The Athletics were successful in Oakland, winning four World Series championships, six American League pennants, and 17 Western Division titles. Despite the team's accomplishments, the Athletics left Oakland after the 2024 season, citing the aging Oakland Coliseum and inability to secure taxpayer funding for a new ballpark in the [East Bay](/wiki/East_Bay "East Bay") or [San Jose](/wiki/San_Jose,_California "San Jose, California") areas. In 2025, the team relocated to [West Sacramento](/wiki/West_Sacramento,_California "West Sacramento, California") as the [Athletics](/wiki/Athletics_(baseball) "Athletics (baseball)"), with an eventual [planned relocation](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics_relocation_to_Las_Vegas "Oakland Athletics relocation to Las Vegas") to the [Las Vegas metropolitan area](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Valley "Las Vegas Valley") on a permanent basis. The move marked the end of professional major league sports in Oakland.

The Oakland Athletics had an overall win–loss record of 4,614–4,387–1 (.513) during their 56 years in Oakland. Seventeen former Oakland Athletics players were elected to the [National Baseball Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame "National Baseball Hall of Fame") with [Dennis Eckersley](/wiki/Dennis_Eckersley "Dennis Eckersley"), [Rollie Fingers](/wiki/Rollie_Fingers "Rollie Fingers"), [Rickey Henderson](/wiki/Rickey_Henderson "Rickey Henderson"), and [Dick Williams](/wiki/Dick_Williams "Dick Williams") depicted with an Oakland Athletics cap.

### Relocation from Kansas City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Relocation from Kansas City")]

In 1954, [Arnold Johnson](/wiki/Arnold_Johnson_(industrialist) "Arnold Johnson (industrialist)") bought the Athletics and moved the team from their original home of [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia_Athletics "Philadelphia Athletics") to [Kansas City, Missouri](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"). After Johnson's death in 1960, the organization was purchased by [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") businessman [Charlie Finley](/wiki/Charlie_Finley "Charlie Finley"), who initially promised to keep the team in their current home.[[4]](#cite_note-Neumann-5)

As early as 1961, Finley sought a new home for the Athletics, including cities such as [Dallas–Fort Worth](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth_metroplex "Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex"), [Louisville](/wiki/Louisville,_Kentucky "Louisville, Kentucky"), [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"), [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego"), and [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle").[[4]](#cite_note-Neumann-5) In January [1964](/wiki/1964_Kansas_City_Athletics_season "1964 Kansas City Athletics season"), he signed an agreement on to move the A's to Louisville,[[5]](#cite_note-atslflm-6) promising to change the team's name to the "Kentucky Athletics".[[6]](#cite_note-7) [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") owners turned it down by a 9–1 margin on January 16, with Finley being the only one voting in favor.[[7]](#cite_note-sogawy-8) Six weeks later, by the same 9–1 margin, the AL owners denied Finley's request to move the team to [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland,_California "Oakland, California"). In agreement with AL President [Joe Cronin](/wiki/Joe_Cronin "Joe Cronin"), Finley signed a four-year lease with [Municipal Stadium](/wiki/Municipal_Stadium_(Kansas_City,_Missouri) "Municipal Stadium (Kansas City, Missouri)") in February 1964.[[8]](#cite_note-sdckcag-9).

During the [World Series](/wiki/1967_World_Series "1967 World Series") on October 11, 1967, Finley announced his choice of Oakland over Seattle as the team's new home.[[9]](#cite_note-10) A week later on October 18 in Chicago, AL owners gave him permission to move the Athletics to Oakland for the [1968](/wiki/1968_Oakland_Athletics_season "1968 Oakland Athletics season") season.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

### A new home and the emergence of a powerhouse (1968–1970)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: A new home and the emergence of a powerhouse (1968–1970)")]

The Athletics' Oakland tenure opened with a 3–1 loss to the [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/Baltimore_Orioles "Baltimore Orioles") on April 10, 1968, and their first game in Oakland was on April 17, a 4–1 loss to the Orioles. They played their home games at the recently opened [Oakland–Alameda County Coliseum](/wiki/Oakland%E2%80%93Alameda_County_Coliseum "Oakland–Alameda County Coliseum"), the home of the [AFL](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League")'s [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders"), with whom they shared the stadium. The Athletics drew national attention when, on May 8, 1968, [Jim "Catfish" Hunter](/wiki/Catfish_Hunter "Catfish Hunter") pitched a [perfect game](/wiki/Perfect_game_(baseball) "Perfect game (baseball)") (the American League's first during the regular season since 1922) against the [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/1968_Minnesota_Twins_season "1968 Minnesota Twins season"). The Athletics, under the leadership of manager [Bob Kennedy](/wiki/Bob_Kennedy "Bob Kennedy"), ended the 1968 campaign with an 82–80 record, their first winning record since [1952](/wiki/1952_Philadelphia_Athletics_season "1952 Philadelphia Athletics season") (in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia")). The team's output also represented a 20-win increase over the [prior year's](/wiki/1967_Kansas_City_Athletics_season "1967 Kansas City Athletics season") 62–99 finish. Bob Kennedy was fired at the end of the season.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

Expansion brought optimism to Athletics fans after AL owners (unlike their counterparts in the National League) decided to realign their league strictly based on geography. Despite finishing in sixth place and only two games above .500 in 1968, Oakland actually had the best record of the four established teams to join the [AL West](/wiki/AL_West "AL West"), which also contained the two expansion teams. The Athletics began the [1969 season](/wiki/1969_Oakland_Athletics_season "1969 Oakland Athletics season") under the leadership of [Hank Bauer](/wiki/Hank_Bauer "Hank Bauer"). On July 20, 1969, future ace [Vida Blue](/wiki/Vida_Blue "Vida Blue") made his major league debut with a start against the [California Angels](/wiki/California_Angels "California Angels"). The Athletics' on-field performance continued to improve; led by [Reggie Jackson](/wiki/Reggie_Jackson "Reggie Jackson")'s 47 home runs, the A's finished the season with a record of 88–74. However, this was only good enough for second place behind the [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/1969_Minnesota_Twins_season "1969 Minnesota Twins season"), and was not good enough for Finley, who had been expecting his team to win the division title. Hank Bauer was fired (and replaced with [John McNamara](/wiki/John_McNamara_(baseball) "John McNamara (baseball)")) near the end of the season. The team's record stood at 80–69 at the time of his firing. McNamara himself would be fired following an 89–73 finish in [1970](/wiki/1970_Oakland_Athletics_season "1970 Oakland Athletics season"). He was replaced by former [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") manager [Dick Williams](/wiki/Dick_Williams "Dick Williams").[[12]](#cite_note-13)

### Swingin' A's (1971–1975)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Swingin' A's (1971–1975)")]

The Athletics, following two consecutive second-place finishes, finally claimed the division crown in [1971](/wiki/1971_Oakland_Athletics_season "1971 Oakland Athletics season"). The A's would win 101 games (their first 100-win season since finishing 107–45 in [1931](/wiki/1931_Philadelphia_Athletics_season "1931 Philadelphia Athletics season")). However, they lost to the [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/Baltimore_Orioles "Baltimore Orioles") in the [American League Championship Series](/wiki/American_League_Championship_Series "American League Championship Series"). In 1972, the A's won their first league pennant since 1931 and faced the [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds "Cincinnati Reds") in the [World Series](/wiki/1972_World_Series "1972 World Series").[[13]](#cite_note-14)

That year, the A's began wearing solid green or solid gold jerseys, with contrasting white pants, at a time when most other teams wore all-white uniforms at home and all-grey ones on the road. Similar to more colorful amateur softball uniforms, they were considered a radical departure for their time. Furthermore, in conjunction with a Moustache Day promotion, Finley offered $300 to any player who grew a moustache by Father's Day, at a time when every other team forbade facial hair. When Father's Day arrived, every member of the team collected a bonus. The [1972 World Series](/wiki/1972_World_Series "1972 World Series") against the Cincinnati Reds was termed "The Hairs vs. the Big Squares", as the Reds wore more traditional uniforms and required their players to be clean-shaven and short-haired. A contemporaneous book about the team was called *Mustache Gang*. The A's seven-game victory over the heavily favored Reds gave the team its first World Series Championship since [1930](/wiki/1930_World_Series "1930 World Series").[[14]](#cite_note-15)

They defended their title in [1973](/wiki/1973_World_Series "1973 World Series") and [1974](/wiki/1974_World_Series "1974 World Series"). Unlike Mack's champions, who thoroughly dominated their opposition, the A's teams of the 1970s played well enough to win their division (which was usually known as the "American League Least" during this time). They then defeated teams that had won more games during the regular season with good pitching, good defense, and clutch hitting. Finley called this team the "Swingin' A's". Players such as [Reggie Jackson](/wiki/Reggie_Jackson "Reggie Jackson"), [Sal Bando](/wiki/Sal_Bando "Sal Bando"), [Joe Rudi](/wiki/Joe_Rudi "Joe Rudi"), [Bert Campaneris](/wiki/Bert_Campaneris "Bert Campaneris"), [Catfish Hunter](/wiki/Catfish_Hunter "Catfish Hunter"), [Rollie Fingers](/wiki/Rollie_Fingers "Rollie Fingers"), and [Vida Blue](/wiki/Vida_Blue "Vida Blue") formed the nucleus of these teams.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The players often said in later years that they played so well as a team because almost to a man, they hated Finley with a passion. For instance, Finley threatened to pack Jackson off to the minors in 1969 after Jackson hit 47 homers; [Commissioner](/wiki/Commissioner_of_Baseball "Commissioner of Baseball") [Bowie Kuhn](/wiki/Bowie_Kuhn "Bowie Kuhn") had to intervene in their contract dispute. Kuhn intervened again after Blue won the AL [Cy Young Award](/wiki/Cy_Young_Award "Cy Young Award") in 1971 and Finley threatened to send him to the minors. Finley's tendency for micromanaging his team actually dated to the team's stay in Kansas City. Among the more notable incidents during this time was a near-mutiny in 1967; Finley responded by releasing the A's best hitter, [Ken Harrelson](/wiki/Ken_Harrelson "Ken Harrelson"), who promptly signed with the Red Sox and helped lead them to [the pennant](/wiki/1967_Boston_Red_Sox_season "1967 Boston Red Sox season").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Athletics' victory over the [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets") in the 1973 Series was marred by Finley's antics. Finley forced [Mike Andrews](/wiki/Mike_Andrews "Mike Andrews") to sign a false affidavit saying he was injured after the reserve second baseman committed two consecutive errors in the 12th inning of the A's Game Two loss to the Mets. When Williams, Andrews' teammates, and virtually the entire viewing public rallied to Andrews' defense, Kuhn forced Finley to back down. However, there was nothing that said the A's had to play Andrews. Andrews entered Game 4 in the eighth inning as a pinch-hitter to a standing ovation from sympathetic Mets fans. He promptly grounded out, and Finley ordered him benched for the remainder of the Series. Andrews never played another major league game. As it was, the incident allowed the Mets, a team that went but 82–79 during the regular season, to stretch the Series to the full seven games against a far superior team. Williams was so disgusted by the affair that he resigned after the Series. Finley retaliated by vetoing Williams' attempt to become manager of the Yankees. Finley claimed that since Williams still owed Oakland the last year of his contract, he could not manage anywhere else. Finley relented later in 1974 and allowed Williams to take over as manager of the [California Angels](/wiki/California_Angels "California Angels").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After the Athletics' victory over the [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Dodgers "Los Angeles Dodgers") in the 1974 Series (under [Alvin Dark](/wiki/Alvin_Dark "Alvin Dark")), pitcher Catfish Hunter filed a grievance, claiming that the team had violated its contract with Hunter by failing to make timely payment on an insurance policy during the 1974 season as called for. On December 13, 1974, arbitrator Peter Seitz ruled in Hunter's favor. As a result, Hunter became a free agent, and signed a contract with the Yankees for the 1975 season. Despite the loss of Hunter, the A's repeated as AL West champions in 1975, but lost the ALCS to Boston in a 3-game sweep.

### Free agency, the dismantling of the A's, and the end of the Finley years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Free agency, the dismantling of the A's, and the end of the Finley years")]

#### 1975

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1975")]

In [1975](/wiki/1975_in_baseball "1975 in baseball"), fed up with poor attendance in Oakland during the team's championship years, Finley thought of moving yet again. When Seattle filed a lawsuit against Major League Baseball over the move of the [Seattle Pilots](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers "Milwaukee Brewers") to Milwaukee, Finley and others came up with an elaborate shuffle which would move the ailing [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox") to Seattle. Finley then would move the A's to Chicago, closer to his home in [LaPorte, Indiana](/wiki/LaPorte,_Indiana "LaPorte, Indiana"); and take the White Sox' place at [Comiskey Park](/wiki/Comiskey_Park "Comiskey Park"). The scheme fell through when White Sox owner [John Allyn](/wiki/John_Allyn "John Allyn") sold the team to another colorful owner, [Bill Veeck](/wiki/Bill_Veeck "Bill Veeck"), who was not interested in leaving Chicago.

#### 1976

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1976")]

As the [1976](/wiki/1976_in_baseball "1976 in baseball") season got underway, the basic rules of player contracts were changing. Seitz had ruled that baseball's reserve clause only bound players for one season after their contract expired. Thus, all players not signed to multi-year contracts would be eligible for free agency at the end of the 1976 season. The balance of power had shifted from the owners to the players for the first time since the days of the Federal League. Like Mack had done twice before, Finley reacted by trading star players and attempting to sell others. On June 15, [1976](/wiki/1976_in_baseball "1976 in baseball"), Finley sold left fielder Rudi and relief pitcher Fingers to Boston for $1 million each, and pitcher Blue to the New York Yankees for $1.5 million. Three days later, Kuhn voided the transactions in the "best interests of baseball". Amid the turmoil, the A's still finished second in the AL West, 2.5 games behind the Royals.

#### 1977

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1977")]

After the 1976 season, most of the Athletics' veteran players did become eligible for free agency, and predictably almost all left. More than 40 years and 3,000 miles (4,800 km) after Connie Mack's last dynasty, one of baseball's most storied franchises suffered yet another dismemberment of a dynasty team. As happened with the end of the A's first dynasty in the early 1910s, the collapse was swift, sudden and total. The next three years were as bad as the worst days in Philadelphia or Kansas City, with the A's finishing last twice and next-to-last once. In [1977](/wiki/1977_in_baseball "1977 in baseball"), for instance—only three years after winning the World Series and two years after playing for the pennant—the A's finished with the worst record in the American League, and the second-worst record in baseball. They even trailed the expansion [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/Seattle_Mariners "Seattle Mariners") (though by only 1⁄2 game, as one game with the [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/Minnesota_Twins "Minnesota Twins") was canceled by weather and never made up).

At the end of the 1977 season, Finley attempted to trade Blue to the Reds for a player of lesser stature and cash, but Kuhn vetoed the deal, claiming that it was tantamount to a fire sale similar to the sales he voided a year earlier. He also claimed that adding Blue to the Reds' already formidable pitching staff would make a mockery of the National League West race. Later, Finley sent [Doug Bair](/wiki/Doug_Bair "Doug Bair") to the Reds in a deal that Kuhn deemed a true trade. At the same time, Blue was traded across the bay to the [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/San_Francisco_Giants "San Francisco Giants") in a multi-player trade that likewise received the Commissioner's blessing.

#### 1978–1980

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1978–1980")]

Despite Finley's reputation as a master promoter, the A's had never drawn well since moving to Oakland, even during the World Series years. In the three years after the veterans from the championship years left, attendance dropped so low that the Coliseum became known as the "Oakland Mausoleum". At one point during the late 1970s, crowds could be counted in the hundreds. The low point came in [1979](/wiki/1979_Oakland_Athletics_season "1979 Oakland Athletics season"), when an April 17 game against the Mariners drew an announced crowd of 653. However, A's officials claimed the actual attendance was 550, while first baseman [Dave Revering](/wiki/Dave_Revering "Dave Revering") thought the crowd was closer to 200. What is beyond dispute is that it was the smallest "crowd" in the West Coast portion of A's history.[[15]](#cite_note-Mad-16) The Coliseum's upkeep also went downhill. The franchise's rapid deterioration so soon after being the most powerful team in the game led some fans to nickname them "the Triple-A's".

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Oakland_Coliseum_diamond_1981.jpg/330px-Oakland_Coliseum_diamond_1981.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oakland_Coliseum_diamond_1981.jpg)

The Oakland Athletics playing host to the [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)") at the Oakland–Alameda County Coliseum during a 1981 home game.

For most of Finley's ownership, the A's rarely had radio or television contracts, rendering them all but invisible in the Bay Area even during the World Series era. For the first month of the 1978 season, the A's broadcast their games on [KALX](/wiki/KALX "KALX"), a 10-watt college radio station run by the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley").[[15]](#cite_note-Mad-16) KALX was practically unlistenable more than 10 miles (16 km) from Oakland. At that time, the A's had a radio network stretching all the way to Hawaii, leading one fan to joke, "[Honolulu](/wiki/City_and_County_of_Honolulu "City and County of Honolulu")? How about *here?*"[[16]](#cite_note-17) In 1979, the A's did not sign a radio contract until the night before opening day. The A's near-invisibility prompted Oakland and [Alameda County](/wiki/Alameda_County "Alameda County") to sue Finley and the A's for [breach of contract](/wiki/Breach_of_contract "Breach of contract") in 1979.[[15]](#cite_note-Mad-16)

Finley nearly sold the team to buyers who would have moved them to [Mile High Stadium](/wiki/Mile_High_Stadium "Mile High Stadium") in [Denver](/wiki/Denver "Denver") for the [1978](/wiki/1978_in_baseball "1978 in baseball") season and the [Louisiana Superdome](/wiki/Louisiana_Superdome "Louisiana Superdome") in [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") for [1979](/wiki/1979_in_baseball "1979 in baseball"). Though the American League owners appeared to favor the Denver deal, it fell through when the city of Oakland and Alameda County refused to release the A's from their lease. At the time, the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders") were threatening to move to Los Angeles, and city and county officials were not willing to lose Oakland's status as a big-league city in its own right. Not surprisingly, only 306,763 paying customers showed up to watch the A's in 1979, the team's worst attendance since leaving Philadelphia.

After three dismal seasons on the field and at the gate, the commissioner's office seriously considered selling the team out from under Finley and moving it to New Orleans. Rather than acquiesce, Finley hired Berkeley native [Billy Martin](/wiki/Billy_Martin "Billy Martin") to manage the young team, led by new young stars [Rickey Henderson](/wiki/Rickey_Henderson "Rickey Henderson"), [Mike Norris](/wiki/Mike_Norris_(baseball) "Mike Norris (baseball)"), [Tony Armas](/wiki/Tony_Armas "Tony Armas"), and [Dwayne Murphy](/wiki/Dwayne_Murphy "Dwayne Murphy"). Martin made believers of his young charges, "Billyball" was used to market the team, and the Athletics finished second in [1980](/wiki/1980_Oakland_Athletics_season "1980 Oakland Athletics season").[[17]](#cite_note-18)

However, during that same season Finley's wife sought a divorce, and would not accept a stake in the A's in a property settlement. With most of his money tied up in the A's or his insurance empire, Finley had to sell the team. He agreed in principle to sell to businessman [Marvin Davis](/wiki/Marvin_Davis "Marvin Davis"), who would have moved the Athletics to Denver. However, just before Finley and Davis were due to sign a definitive agreement, the Raiders announced their move to Los Angeles. Oakland and Alameda County officials let it be known that they would not allow any prospective owner to break the Coliseum lease, forcing Davis to call off the deal. Forced to turn to local buyers, Finley sold the A's to San Francisco clothing manufacturer [Walter A. Haas, Jr.](/wiki/Walter_A._Haas,_Jr. "Walter A. Haas, Jr."), president of [Levi Strauss & Co.](/wiki/Levi_Strauss_%26_Co. "Levi Strauss & Co.") prior to the [1981](/wiki/1981_in_baseball "1981 in baseball") season. It would not be the last time that the Raiders directly affected the A's future; Denver would eventually get an MLB team in [1993](/wiki/1993_MLB_season "1993 MLB season") when the [Colorado Rockies](/wiki/Colorado_Rockies "Colorado Rockies") began play.

### Local ownership for the Athletics: the Haas era (1981–1995)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Local ownership for the Athletics: the Haas era (1981–1995)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Oakland_Athletics_logo_1983_to_1992.png/250px-Oakland_Athletics_logo_1983_to_1992.png)](/wiki/File:Oakland_Athletics_logo_1983_to_1992.png)

The Oakland Athletics logo (1983–1992)

Despite winning three World Series and two other AL West Division titles, the A's on-field success did not translate into success at the box office during the Finley era in Oakland. Average home attendance from 1968 to 1980 was 777,000 per season, with 1,075,518 in 1975 being the highest attendance for a Finley-owned team. In marked contrast, during the first year of Haas' ownership, the Athletics drew 1,304,052—in a season shortened by a player strike. Were it not for the strike, the A's were on a pace to draw over 2.2 million in 1981. This lent credence to the theory that Bay Area residents stayed away from the Coliseum because they did not want to give their money to Finley.[[15]](#cite_note-Mad-16)

Haas set about changing the team's image. He ditched Charlie O. as the team mascot and restored the traditional team name of "Athletics" as soon as he closed on the purchase, with the ownership group formally known as "The Oakland Athletics Baseball Company". He also installed pictures of Connie Mack and other greats from the Philadelphia days in the team office; Finley had scarcely acknowledged the team's past. While the team colors remained green, gold, and white, the bright Kelly green was replaced with a more subdued forest green. After a 23-year hiatus, the elephant was restored as the club mascot in 1988. The script "Athletics", which had adorned home and road jerseys from 1954 to 1960, was returned to home jerseys in 1987.

The Haases gave Martin complete control of the baseball operation with the title of "player development director", effectively making him his own general manager. The A's lost in the American League Championship Series after winning the "first half" AL West Division title of the strike-interrupted [1981](/wiki/1981_in_baseball "1981 in baseball") season. The club finished with the second-best overall record in baseball, and the best record in the American League. Had the season not been split in half, the 1981 A's would have gone wire-to-wire. However, an injury-riddled team significantly regressed in 1982, falling to 68–94. Although Martin was not blamed for the debacle, growing concern about his off-field behavior resulted in his firing after the season.

During the 15 years of Haas' ownership, the Athletics became one of baseball's most successful teams at the gate, drawing 2,900,217 in 1990, still the club record for single season attendance, as well as on the field. Average annual home attendance during those years (excluding the strike years of 1981 and 1994) was over 1.9 million.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/1985_Mother%27s_Cookies_-_Oakland_Coliseum.JPG/330px-1985_Mother%27s_Cookies_-_Oakland_Coliseum.JPG)](/wiki/File:1985_Mother%27s_Cookies_-_Oakland_Coliseum.JPG)

The Oakland Athletics hosting a home game in 1985

Under the Haas ownership, the minor league system was rebuilt, which bore fruit later that decade as [José Canseco](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Canseco "José Canseco") (1986), [Mark McGwire](/wiki/Mark_McGwire "Mark McGwire") (1987), and [Walt Weiss](/wiki/Walt_Weiss "Walt Weiss") (1988) were chosen as [AL Rookies of the Year](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_Rookie_of_the_Year_Award "Major League Baseball Rookie of the Year Award"). During the 1986 season, [Tony La Russa](/wiki/Tony_La_Russa "Tony La Russa") was hired as the Athletics' manager, a post he held until the end of 1995. In 1987, La Russa's first full year as manager, the team finished at 81–81, its best record in seven seasons. Beginning in 1988, the Athletics won the AL pennant three years in a row. Reminiscent of their Philadelphia predecessors, this A's team finished with the best record of any team in the major leagues during all 3 years, winning 104 (1988), 99 (1989), and 103 (1990) games, featuring such stars as McGwire, Canseco, Weiss, Rickey Henderson, [Carney Lansford](/wiki/Carney_Lansford "Carney Lansford"), [Dave Stewart](/wiki/Dave_Stewart_(baseball) "Dave Stewart (baseball)"), and [Dennis Eckersley](/wiki/Dennis_Eckersley "Dennis Eckersley").

During this time, [Rickey Henderson](/wiki/Rickey_Henderson "Rickey Henderson") shattered [Lou Brock](/wiki/Lou_Brock "Lou Brock")'s modern major league record by stealing 130 [bases](/wiki/Stolen_base "Stolen base") in a single season (1982), a total which has not been approached since. On May 1, 1991, Henderson broke one of baseball's most famous records when he stole the 939th base of his career, one more than Brock.

Regular season dominance led to some success in the post-season. The Athletics' lone World Series championship of the era was a four-game sweep of the [cross-bay rival](/wiki/Bay_Bridge_Series "Bay Bridge Series") [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/San_Francisco_Giants "San Francisco Giants") in the [1989 World Series](/wiki/1989_World_Series "1989 World Series"). Unfortunately for the A's, their sweep of the Giants was overshadowed by the [Loma Prieta earthquake](/wiki/Loma_Prieta_earthquake "Loma Prieta earthquake") that occurred at the start of Game 3 before a national television audience. This forced the remaining games to be delayed for ten days. When play resumed, the atmosphere was dominated more by a sense of relief than celebration by baseball fans. Heavily favored Athletics teams lost the World Series in both [1988](/wiki/1988_World_Series "1988 World Series"), to the [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Dodgers "Los Angeles Dodgers"), and in [1990](/wiki/1990_World_Series "1990 World Series"), to the [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds "Cincinnati Reds"). The latter was a shocking four-game sweep reminiscent of the A's loss to the [Boston Braves](/wiki/Boston_Braves_(baseball) "Boston Braves (baseball)") 76 years earlier. The team began declining, winning the AL West championship in 1992 (but losing to [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto_Blue_Jays "Toronto Blue Jays") in the ALCS), then finishing last in 1993.

### The "Moneyball" years (1996–2004)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: The \"Moneyball\" years (1996–2004)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Oakland_A%27s_logo.svg/250px-Oakland_A%27s_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Oakland_A%27s_logo.svg)

Final Oakland A's logo (1993–2024)

In 1995, the Raiders returned to Oakland after spending 12 years in Los Angeles; with this, the Coliseum underwent an $83 million facelift that altered the Coliseum significantly.[[18]](#cite_note-19) Walter Haas died in that same year, and the team was sold to San Francisco Bay Area real estate developers [Steve Schott](/wiki/Stephen_Schott "Stephen Schott") (third cousin to one-time Cincinnati Reds' owner [Marge Schott](/wiki/Marge_Schott "Marge Schott")), silent partner David Etheridge and [Ken Hofmann](/wiki/Ken_Hofmann "Ken Hofmann"), prior to the 1996 season. Once again, the Athletics' star players were traded or sold, as the new owners' goal was to cut payroll drastically. Many landed with the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals"), including McGwire, Eckersley, and manager La Russa. In a turn of events eerily reminiscent of the A's [Roger Maris](/wiki/Roger_Maris "Roger Maris") trade 38 years before, Mark McGwire celebrated his first full season with the Cardinals by setting a new major league home run record.

The Schott-Hofmann ownership allocated resources to building and maintaining a strong minor league system while almost always refusing to pay the going rate to keep star players on the team once they become free agents. Perhaps as a result, at the turn of the 21st century, the A's were a team that usually finished at or near the top of the AL West Division, but could not advance beyond the first round of the playoffs. The Athletics made the playoffs for four straight years, from 2000 to 2003, but lost their first round (best three-out-of-five) series in each case, 3 games to 2. In two of those years (2001 against the Yankees and 2003 against the Red Sox), the Athletics won the first two games of the series, only to lose the next three straight. In 2001, Oakland became the first team to lose a best-of-five series after winning both of the first two games on the road. In 2004, the A's missed the playoffs altogether, losing the final series of the season—and the divisional title—to the [Anaheim Angels](/wiki/Anaheim_Angels "Anaheim Angels") by one game.

This period in Oakland history featured splendid performances from a trio of young starting pitchers: right-hander [Tim Hudson](/wiki/Tim_Hudson "Tim Hudson") and left-handers [Mark Mulder](/wiki/Mark_Mulder "Mark Mulder") and [Barry Zito](/wiki/Barry_Zito "Barry Zito"). Between 1999 and 2006, the so-called "Big Three" helped the Athletics to emerge into a perennial powerhouse in the American League West, combining for a collective record of 261–131. They gave the Athletics a 1–2–3 punch to add to talented infielders and potent hitters, such as first baseman [Jason Giambi](/wiki/Jason_Giambi "Jason Giambi"), shortstop [Miguel Tejada](/wiki/Miguel_Tejada "Miguel Tejada"), and third baseman [Eric Chavez](/wiki/Eric_Chavez "Eric Chavez"). Giambi was named [American League MVP](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "Major League Baseball Most Valuable Player Award") in 2000, and Tejada won an MVP Award of his own in 2002, a year which also saw Zito win 23 games and the [Cy Young Award](/wiki/Cy_Young_Award "Cy Young Award").

On May 29, 2000, [Randy Velarde](/wiki/Randy_Velarde "Randy Velarde") achieved an [unassisted triple play](/wiki/Unassisted_triple_play "Unassisted triple play") against the Yankees. In the sixth, second baseman Velarde caught [Shane Spencer](/wiki/Shane_Spencer "Shane Spencer")'s line drive, tagged [Jorge Posada](/wiki/Jorge_Posada "Jorge Posada") running from first to second, and stepped on second before [Tino Martinez](/wiki/Tino_Martinez "Tino Martinez") could return. (Velarde had also pulled off an unassisted triple play during a spring training game that year). This was only the 11th unassisted triple play in the history of Major League Baseball.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d0/Billy_Beane_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Billy_Beane_2006.jpg)

Billy Beane in 2006.

The general manager of the Athletics at the time, [Billy Beane](/wiki/Billy_Beane "Billy Beane"), became notable due to [Michael Lewis](/wiki/Michael_Lewis_(author) "Michael Lewis (author)")'s book portrayal of Beane's novel approach to business decisions and scouting, referred to as [Moneyball](/wiki/Moneyball:_The_Art_of_Winning_an_Unfair_Game "Moneyball: The Art of Winning an Unfair Game"). Through Beane, the Athletics organization redefined the way major league baseball teams evaluate player talent. They began filling their system with players who did not possess traditionally valued baseball "tools" of throwing, fielding, hitting, hitting for power and running. Instead, they drafted for unconventional statistical prowess: on-base percentage for hitters (rather than batting average) and strikeout/walk ratios for pitchers (rather than velocity). These undervalued stats came cheaply. With the sixth-lowest payroll in baseball in 2002, the Oakland Athletics won an American League best 103 games. They spent $41 million that season, while the Yankees, who also won 103 games, spent $126 million.

The Athletics continually succeeded at winning, and defying market economics, keeping their payroll near the bottom of the league. After the 2004 season, in which the A's placed second in their division, Beane shocked many by breaking up the Big Three, trading Tim Hudson to the [Atlanta Braves](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves "Atlanta Braves") and Mark Mulder to the St. Louis Cardinals. To many, the trades appeared bizarre, in that the two pitchers were seen to be at or near the top of their game; however, the decision was perfectly in line with Beane's business model. The Mulder trade, to many experts' surprise, turned into a steal for the Athletics, as little-known starter [Dan Haren](/wiki/Dan_Haren "Dan Haren") ended up pitching far better for Oakland than Mulder did for St. Louis.

Also during this time, the Athletics won an [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") record 20 games in a row, from August 13 to September 4, 2002. The last three games were won in dramatic fashion, each victory coming in the bottom of the ninth inning. Win number 20 was notable because the A's, with Tim Hudson pitching, jumped to an 11–0 lead against the AL-cellar dwelling [Kansas City Royals](/wiki/Kansas_City_Royals "Kansas City Royals"), only to slowly give up 11 unanswered runs to lose the lead. Then, [Scott Hatteberg](/wiki/Scott_Hatteberg "Scott Hatteberg"), enduring criticism as Jason Giambi's replacement, hit a pinch-hit [walk-off home run](/wiki/Walk-off_home_run "Walk-off home run") off Royals closer [Jason Grimsley](/wiki/Jason_Grimsley "Jason Grimsley") in the bottom of the 9th inning to win 12–11. The streak was snapped two nights later in Minneapolis, with the A's losing 6–0 to the [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/Minnesota_Twins "Minnesota Twins").

### The Wolff era (2005–2016)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: The Wolff era (2005–2016)")]

#### 2005

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 2005")]

On March 30, [2005](/wiki/2005_Major_League_Baseball_season "2005 Major League Baseball season"), the Athletics were sold to a group fronted by real estate developer [Lewis Wolff](/wiki/Lewis_Wolff "Lewis Wolff"), although the majority owner is [John J. Fisher](/wiki/John_J._Fisher "John J. Fisher"), son of [The Gap, Inc.](/wiki/The_Gap,_Inc. "The Gap, Inc.")'s founder. Wolff, though a [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") businessman, had successfully developed many real estate projects in and around [San Jose](/wiki/San_Jose,_California "San Jose, California"). The previous ownership had retained Wolff to help them find an adequate parcel on which to construct a new stadium. Because of Wolff's background, rumors that he wanted to move the team to San Jose surfaced periodically upon his purchase of the team. However, any such plans were always complicated by the claims of the cross-bay [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/San_Francisco_Giants "San Francisco Giants") that they own the territorial rights to San Jose and [Santa Clara County](/wiki/Santa_Clara_County,_California "Santa Clara County, California").

In [2005](/wiki/2005_in_baseball "2005 in baseball"), many pundits picked the Athletics to finish last as a result of Beane's dismantling of the Big Three. At first, the experts appeared vindicated, as the A's were mired in last place on May 31 with a 19–32 (.373) win–loss record. After that the team began to gel, playing at a .622 clip for the remainder of the season, eventually finishing 88–74 (.543), seven games behind the newly renamed [Los Angeles Angels of Anaheim](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Angels_of_Anaheim "Los Angeles Angels of Anaheim") and for many weeks seriously contending for the AL West crown.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Game_1_2006ALCS.JPG/250px-Game_1_2006ALCS.JPG)](/wiki/File:Game_1_2006ALCS.JPG)

Game 1 of the 2006 ALCS in [Oakland, California](/wiki/Oakland,_California "Oakland, California")

Pitcher [Huston Street](/wiki/Huston_Street "Huston Street") was voted the AL [Rookie of the Year](/wiki/MLB_Rookie_of_the_Year_Award "MLB Rookie of the Year Award") in 2005, the second year in a row an Athletic won that award, shortstop [Bobby Crosby](/wiki/Bobby_Crosby "Bobby Crosby") having won in 2004. For the fifth straight season, third baseman [Eric Chavez](/wiki/Eric_Chavez "Eric Chavez") won the AL [Gold Glove Award](/wiki/Gold_Glove_Award "Gold Glove Award") at that position.

#### 2006

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2006")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Rally_Sticks.JPG/120px-Rally_Sticks.JPG)](/wiki/File:Rally_Sticks.JPG)

Rally sticks given to fans for Games 1 and 2 of the ALCS in Oakland

The [2006](/wiki/2006_Major_League_Baseball_season "2006 Major League Baseball season") season brought the A's back to the postseason after a three-year absence. After finishing the season at 93–69, four games ahead of the [Angels](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Angels_of_Anaheim "Los Angeles Angels of Anaheim"), the A's were considered the underdog against the highly favored [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/Minnesota_Twins "Minnesota Twins"). The A's swept the series 3–0 however, despite having to start on the road and losing second baseman [Mark Ellis](/wiki/Mark_Ellis_(baseball) "Mark Ellis (baseball)"), who sustained a broken finger after getting hit by a pitch in the second game. Their victory was short-lived though, as the A's were swept 4–0 by the [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers"). Manager [Ken Macha](/wiki/Ken_Macha "Ken Macha") was fired by [Billy Beane](/wiki/Billy_Beane "Billy Beane") on October 16, four days after their loss in the [2006 American League Championship Series](/wiki/2006_American_League_Championship_Series "2006 American League Championship Series"). Beane cited a disconnect between him and his players as well as a general unhappiness among the team as the reason for his sudden departure.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

Macha was replaced by bench coach and former major league catcher [Bob Geren](/wiki/Bob_Geren "Bob Geren"). Following the 2006 season, the A's also lost ace [Barry Zito](/wiki/Barry_Zito "Barry Zito") to the [Giants](/wiki/San_Francisco_Giants "San Francisco Giants") due to free agency. They also lost their DH and MVP candidate [Frank Thomas](/wiki/Frank_E._Thomas "Frank E. Thomas") to free agency but filled his role with [Mike Piazza](/wiki/Mike_Piazza "Mike Piazza") for 2007. Piazza, a lifetime [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") player, agreed to become a full-time DH for the first time in his career.

#### 2007

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 2007")]

The 2007 season was a disappointing season for the A's as they suffered from injuries to several key players [Rich Harden](/wiki/Rich_Harden "Rich Harden"), [Huston Street](/wiki/Huston_Street "Huston Street"), [Eric Chavez](/wiki/Eric_Chavez "Eric Chavez"), and [Mike Piazza](/wiki/Mike_Piazza "Mike Piazza"). For the first time since the 1998 season, the A's finished with a losing record.

The Athletics signed international free agent [Michael Inoa](/wiki/Michael_Inoa "Michael Inoa") to the largest bonus in team and international free agent history.

#### 2008

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 2008")]

The 2008 off-season started with controversy, as the A's traded ace pitcher [Dan Haren](/wiki/Dan_Haren "Dan Haren") to the [Arizona Diamondbacks](/wiki/Arizona_Diamondbacks "Arizona Diamondbacks") for prospects. This would be followed by trades of outfielder [Nick Swisher](/wiki/Nick_Swisher "Nick Swisher"), who was considered to be a fan-favorite, to the [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox"), and another fan-favorite [Mark Kotsay](/wiki/Mark_Kotsay "Mark Kotsay") (also outfielder) to the [Atlanta Braves](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves "Atlanta Braves"). The trades, especially the first two, caused a lot of anger among fans and the media. The A's were considered to be a "rebuilding" team and were expected to be among the bottom-feeders of MLB in the 2008 season. However, the A's performed well into late May, and even held first place in the AL West for a good amount of time, but a 2–7 roadtrip in mid-May allowed the Los Angeles Angels of Anaheim to take first place.

On April 24, just weeks after playing against them while on the Blue Jays, Frank Thomas re-signed with the A's, having been released by the Jays after a slow start. On July 8, the A's were involved in a blockbuster trade, dealing [Rich Harden](/wiki/Rich_Harden "Rich Harden") and [Chad Gaudin](/wiki/Chad_Gaudin "Chad Gaudin") to the [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs") for [Sean Gallagher](/wiki/Sean_Gallagher_(baseball) "Sean Gallagher (baseball)"), Josh Donaldson, [Eric Patterson](/wiki/Eric_Patterson_(baseball) "Eric Patterson (baseball)"), and [Matt Murton](/wiki/Matt_Murton "Matt Murton"). Then on July 17, the A's traded [Joe Blanton](/wiki/Joe_Blanton "Joe Blanton") to the [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies") for three minor leaguers. An 18–37 record for the months of July and August (including a 10-game losing streak) dropped the A's into third place, where they would finish the season. They ended 2008 with a disappointing 75–86 record.

Several players were acquired in the offseason trades (pitchers [Dana Eveland](/wiki/Dana_Eveland "Dana Eveland") and [Greg Smith](/wiki/Greg_Smith_(pitcher) "Greg Smith (pitcher)") from the Dan Haren trade, outfielder [Ryan Sweeney](/wiki/Ryan_Sweeney_(baseball) "Ryan Sweeney (baseball)") from the Swisher trade and reliever [Joey Devine](/wiki/Joey_Devine "Joey Devine") from the Mark Kotsay trade). [Carlos González](/wiki/Carlos_Gonz%C3%A1lez_(baseball) "Carlos González (baseball)") and [Gio González](/wiki/Gio_Gonz%C3%A1lez "Gio González") (no relation) from the Haren and Swisher trades, respectively, also performed well for the Triple-A [Sacramento River Cats](/wiki/Sacramento_River_Cats "Sacramento River Cats"). It is worth pointing out that Haren, Swisher, and Kotsay have all played well in their new teams. Kotsay himself had a game-winning RBI as a pinch-hitter, against his former team on May 16 in Game 1 of an interleague series between the A's and Braves.

#### 2009

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 2009")]

In the 2009 offseason, the A's traded promising young star OF [Carlos González](/wiki/Carlos_Gonz%C3%A1lez_(baseball) "Carlos González (baseball)"), closer [Huston Street](/wiki/Huston_Street "Huston Street") and starting pitcher [Greg Smith](/wiki/Greg_Smith_(pitcher) "Greg Smith (pitcher)") for [Matt Holliday](/wiki/Matt_Holliday "Matt Holliday") of the [Colorado Rockies](/wiki/Colorado_Rockies "Colorado Rockies"). On January 6, 2009, [Jason Giambi](/wiki/Jason_Giambi "Jason Giambi") signed a one-year, $4.6 million contract with a 2nd year option. Giambi said he was glad to be back as he put on his old number 16. Also signed were infielders [Orlando Cabrera](/wiki/Orlando_Cabrera "Orlando Cabrera") of the Chicago White Sox and [Nomar Garciaparra](/wiki/Nomar_Garciaparra "Nomar Garciaparra") of the Los Angeles Dodgers. The first half of the season the team played relatively poor, but finished the second half strong, yet still posting a losing record. Holliday was dealt to the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals") for prospects and Giambi was released in August after spending time on the DL.

On December 22, 2009, *Sports Illustrated* named general manager [Billy Beane](/wiki/Billy_Beane "Billy Beane") as number 10 on its list of the [Top 10 GMs/Executives of the Decade](/wiki/List_of_2009_all-decade_Sports_Illustrated_awards_and_honors#Top_10_GMs/Executives_of_the_Decade "List of 2009 all-decade Sports Illustrated awards and honors") (in all sports).[[20]](#cite_note-21)

#### 2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 2010")]

The offseason was busy from the start. The team dealt the key-player from the Holliday trade, [Brett Wallace](/wiki/Brett_Wallace "Brett Wallace"), to the [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/Toronto_Blue_Jays "Toronto Blue Jays") for OF [Michael Taylor](/wiki/Michael_Taylor_(baseball,_born_1985) "Michael Taylor (baseball, born 1985)"). After missing all of the [2009](/wiki/2009_Major_League_Baseball_season "2009 Major League Baseball season") season, [Ben Sheets](/wiki/Ben_Sheets "Ben Sheets") signed a 1-year deal. The team had a decent spring, posting a better record than other AL West teams. To begin the regular season, the team had 2 walk-off wins.

On May 9, 42 years almost to the day after Catfish Hunter, A's pitcher [Dallas Braden](/wiki/Dallas_Braden "Dallas Braden") pitched a perfect game, the 19th in Major League history, in a 4–0 victory over the [Tampa Bay Rays](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Rays "Tampa Bay Rays") at the Coliseum. The next homestand was a week-long celebration of the feat, with a commemorative graphic placed on the outfield wall on May 17.

Oakland finished the 2010 season with an 81–81 record; 2nd in the division, 9 games behind Texas, and 1 game ahead of Los Angeles.

#### 2011

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: 2011")]

Oakland finished the 2011 season with a 74–88 record; 3rd in the division, 22 games behind Texas. Pitcher Rich Harden returned on a one-year deal. [Hideki Matsui](/wiki/Hideki_Matsui "Hideki Matsui") was signed as a DH on a one-year deal. [Vin Mazarro](/w/index.php?title=Vin_Mazarro&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vin Mazarro (page does not exist)") was traded to the Royals for [David DeJesus](/wiki/David_DeJesus "David DeJesus"). Travis Buck, Jack Cust, and Edwin Encarnación were lost to the Indians, Mariners, and Blue Jays (respectively). Encarnacion was later claimed off waivers. Rajai Davis was traded to Toronto for two pitchers. Eric Chavez was lost to the Yankees as a free agent.

#### 2012

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 2012")]

After an offseason that saw All Star pitchers [Gio González](/wiki/Gio_Gonz%C3%A1lez "Gio González"), [Trevor Cahill](/wiki/Trevor_Cahill "Trevor Cahill"), and [Andrew Bailey](/wiki/Andrew_Bailey_(baseball) "Andrew Bailey (baseball)") traded away, the A's entered the 2012 season with low expectations. This season was [Bob Melvin](/wiki/Bob_Melvin "Bob Melvin")'s first full season as the A's manager. During the trading period, the A's had traded fan-favorite catcher [Kurt Suzuki](/wiki/Kurt_Suzuki "Kurt Suzuki") to the [Washington Nationals](/wiki/Washington_Nationals "Washington Nationals") for cash considerations. The A's also traded relief pitcher [Fautino de los Santos](/wiki/Fautino_de_los_Santos "Fautino de los Santos") to the [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers "Milwaukee Brewers") for catcher [George Kottaras](/wiki/George_Kottaras "George Kottaras"). On August 15, veteran starting pitcher [Bartolo Colón](/wiki/Bartolo_Col%C3%B3n "Bartolo Colón") received a 50-game suspension after testing positive for performing-enhancing drugs. On September 5, veteran pitcher [Brandon McCarthy](/wiki/Brandon_McCarthy "Brandon McCarthy") was struck in the head by a line drive off of the bat of [Erick Aybar](/wiki/Erick_Aybar "Erick Aybar") ending his 2012 season. The A's entered the last month of the season with an all-rookie starting rotation, but by the end of the month, they had pulled within 2 games of the [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)") for the AL West lead, setting the stage for a season ending, 3-game series that would decide the winner of the 2012 division title. The A's swept the series, culminating in 12–5 victory which saw the A's come back from a 4-run deficit to clinch the AL West for the first time since 2006. The A's ended the regular season with a record of 94–68, leading the Major Leagues in walk-off wins, with 14 in the regular season, and one in Game 4 of the American League Division Series. The A's lost the ALDS to the eventual American League Champion [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers") in 5 games.

Bob Melvin was awarded the 2012 AL Manager of the Year award, and outfielder [Josh Reddick](/wiki/Josh_Reddick "Josh Reddick") was awarded a Gold Glove, becoming the first A's outfielder since 1985 to do so. Following the season shortstop [Cliff Pennington](/wiki/Cliff_Pennington_(baseball) "Cliff Pennington (baseball)") was traded to the [Arizona Diamondbacks](/wiki/Arizona_Diamondbacks "Arizona Diamondbacks") for outfielder [Chris Young](/wiki/Chris_Young_(outfielder) "Chris Young (outfielder)"), as part of a 3 team trade.

#### 2013

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: 2013")]

In 2013, under manager [Bob Melvin](/wiki/Bob_Melvin "Bob Melvin") after going 96–66 and claiming their second straight division title over the heavily favored [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)") and [Los Angeles Angels](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Angels "Los Angeles Angels"), the Athletics lost Game 5 of the ALDS to Justin Verlander and the Detroit Tigers for the second straight season in their own ballpark. [Josh Donaldson](/wiki/Josh_Donaldson "Josh Donaldson") had an MVP-caliber season, with a .301 batting average 24 home runs, and 93 RBIs. Despite his age, Bartolo Colón was in contention for a [Cy Young Award](/wiki/Cy_Young_Award "Cy Young Award"), going 18–6, with 117 [strikeouts](/wiki/Strikeouts "Strikeouts") and a 2.65 [ERA](/wiki/Earned_run_average "Earned run average"). During the regular season the A's saw the additions of former A's catcher [Kurt Suzuki](/wiki/Kurt_Suzuki "Kurt Suzuki") from the [Washington Nationals](/wiki/Washington_Nationals "Washington Nationals"), [Alberto Callaspo](/wiki/Alberto_Callaspo "Alberto Callaspo") from the Los Angeles Angels, and [Stephen Vogt](/wiki/Stephen_Vogt "Stephen Vogt") off waivers from the [Tampa Bay Rays](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Rays "Tampa Bay Rays").[Grant Balfour](/wiki/Grant_Balfour "Grant Balfour") broke the A's record for most consecutive saves and the A's saw the growth of young players like [Jed Lowrie](/wiki/Jed_Lowrie "Jed Lowrie"), [Yoenis Céspedes](/wiki/Yoenis_C%C3%A9spedes "Yoenis Céspedes"), [Josh Donaldson](/wiki/Josh_Donaldson "Josh Donaldson"), and [Sonny Gray](/wiki/Sonny_Gray "Sonny Gray"). The A's would finish in the top 3 of the [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") in [home runs](/wiki/Home_runs "Home runs") and OPS. Even after the devastating loss to the Detroit Tigers, the A's retained most of their 2013 roster, only losing Colon and Balfour to free agency.

#### 2014

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: 2014")]

The A's started out as a favorite to win the AL West again, and played up to that, having the best record in baseball at the All-Star break. To bolster their starting rotation, they acquired pitchers [Jeff Samardzija](/wiki/Jeff_Samardzija "Jeff Samardzija") and [Jason Hammel](/wiki/Jason_Hammel "Jason Hammel") from the [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs") for several top prospects on July 4, and later acquired [Jon Lester](/wiki/Jon_Lester "Jon Lester") from the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") at the July 31 non-waiver trade deadline for [Yoenis Céspedes](/wiki/Yoenis_C%C3%A9spedes "Yoenis Céspedes"). However, without the big bat of Cespedes, poor production from All-Stars [Josh Donaldson](/wiki/Josh_Donaldson "Josh Donaldson") and [Brandon Moss](/wiki/Brandon_Moss "Brandon Moss"), and an injury to closer [Sean Doolittle](/wiki/Sean_Doolittle "Sean Doolittle"), they struggled in August and the [Los Angeles Angels of Anaheim](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Angels_of_Anaheim "Los Angeles Angels of Anaheim") caught up, sweeping them in a key 4-game series between the two teams. At the August 31 waiver trade deadline, the A's acquired first baseman [Adam Dunn](/wiki/Adam_Dunn "Adam Dunn") from the [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox") and cash considerations for a minor league player. Despite their struggles which continued into September, they made the playoffs on the last day of the season, and faced the [Kansas City Royals](/wiki/Kansas_City_Royals "Kansas City Royals") in the [Wild Card Game](/wiki/2014_American_League_Wild_Card_Game "2014 American League Wild Card Game"). While they carried a 7–3 lead going into the bottom of the 8th inning, they managed to relinquish it due to the Royals' baserunning skills coupled with ineffective pitching, allowing them to tie the game. They did regain the lead in the top of the 12th, but the Royals responded with 2 runs in the bottom of the inning, winning on a walk-off single by [Salvador Pérez](/wiki/Salvador_P%C3%A9rez "Salvador Pérez").

#### 2015

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: 2015")]

In the off-season the A's started to rebuild with trading Josh Donaldson, Jeff Samardzija, Brandon Moss, and losing Jon Lester to free agency.

The A's ended 2015 with a disappointing 68–94 record that put them in last place in the AL West. This despite Sonny Gray emerging as an ace of the staff with a 2.73 ERA and 14 wins in 31 starts. Nevertheless, the team brought in future key pieces in [Marcus Semien](/wiki/Marcus_Semien "Marcus Semien"), [Mark Canha](/wiki/Mark_Canha "Mark Canha") and [Chris Bassitt](/wiki/Chris_Bassitt "Chris Bassitt"), all of whom would become integral to the A's success later in the decade.

### The John J. Fisher years (2016–2024)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: The John J. Fisher years (2016–2024)")]

#### 2016

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: 2016")]

Like in 2015, the A's were in last place with a 69–93 record, despite a breakout season from newcomer [Khris Davis](/wiki/Khris_Davis "Khris Davis"), whose 42 home runs began a three-year stretch of 40 home run seasons. New arrivals in [Sean Manaea](/wiki/Sean_Manaea "Sean Manaea") and [Liam Hendriks](/wiki/Liam_Hendriks "Liam Hendriks") later became key pieces on the pitching staff moving forward. In November 2016, after the season ended, Wolff sold his 10% stake in the team to John J. Fisher, who became the full owner of the team; Wolff is now the chairman emeritus.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

#### 2017

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: 2017")]

For the third straight year, the A's were in last place with a 75–87 record. The A's traded away ace Sonny Gray to the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") midway through the season, but brought in struggling reliever [Blake Treinen](/wiki/Blake_Treinen "Blake Treinen") from the [Washington Nationals](/wiki/Washington_Nationals "Washington Nationals"). In addition, [Matt Chapman](/wiki/Matt_Chapman_(baseball) "Matt Chapman (baseball)") was called up as the third baseman of the future.

The team won 10 of their last 14 games, and rookie [Matt Olson](/wiki/Matt_Olson "Matt Olson") hit 24 home runs in just 189 at bats, finishing 4th in [AL Rookie of the Year](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_Rookie_of_the_Year_Award "Major League Baseball Rookie of the Year Award") voting.

#### 2018

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: 2018")]

On April 21, [Sean Manaea](/wiki/Sean_Manaea "Sean Manaea") threw the Athletics franchise's 12th [no-hitter](/wiki/No-hitter "No-hitter"), and their first since [Dallas Braden](/wiki/Dallas_Braden "Dallas Braden") in [2010](/wiki/Dallas_Braden%27s_perfect_game "Dallas Braden's perfect game").[[22]](#cite_note-23)

The A's surprised the American League by winning 97 games, and earned a trip to the postseason as a wild card team. [Bob Melvin](/wiki/Bob_Melvin "Bob Melvin") became the [American League Manager of the Year](/wiki/American_League_Manager_of_the_Year "American League Manager of the Year") for the third time in his coaching career.

#### 2019

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: 2019")]

On May 7 versus the Cincinnati Reds at the [RingCentral Coliseum](/wiki/RingCentral_Coliseum "RingCentral Coliseum"), [Mike Fiers](/wiki/Mike_Fiers "Mike Fiers") threw the Athletics franchise's 13th [no-hitter](/wiki/No-hitter "No-hitter"). It was the second no-hitter of his career, and the 300th no-hitter in MLB history. To bolster the pitching rotation, on July 14, the Athletics acquired RHP [Homer Bailey](/wiki/Homer_Bailey "Homer Bailey") from the [Kansas City Royals](/wiki/Kansas_City_Royals "Kansas City Royals") in exchange for SS Kevin Merrell. On July 27, to improve on their lack-luster bullpen, the Athletics acquired LHP [Jake Diekman](/wiki/Jake_Diekman "Jake Diekman") for OF [Dairon Blanco](/wiki/Dairon_Blanco_(baseball) "Dairon Blanco (baseball)") and RHP [Ismael Aquino](/w/index.php?title=Ismael_Aquino&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ismael Aquino (page does not exist)"). For the second consecutive season, the A's won 97 games and a playoff berth, earning the right to host the Tampa Bay Rays in the American League Wild Card game at Oakland Coliseum on October 2, 2019.[[23]](#cite_note-24) The A's were 52–27 at home on the season.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

#### 2020

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: 2020")]

The Athletics finished with a 36–24 record in the shortened [2020 Major League Baseball season](/wiki/2020_Major_League_Baseball_season "2020 Major League Baseball season"). The A's beat the [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox") two games to one in the [first round](/wiki/2020_American_League_Wild_Card_Series "2020 American League Wild Card Series") of the expanded MLB postseason to face the [Houston Astros](/wiki/Houston_Astros "Houston Astros"). The Athletics lost to the Astros three games to one in the [Division Series](/wiki/2021_American_League_Division_Series "2021 American League Division Series").

#### 2021

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: 2021")]

In 2021, the Athletics finished third the AL West with an 86–76 record, missing the playoffs for the first time since 2017. Following the season, longtime manager Bob Melvin left the organization to become the manager of the San Diego Padres.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

On May 11, 2021, Major League Baseball granted the Athletics permission to explore relocation, saying that the Oakland Coliseum "is not a viable option for the future vision of baseball".[[26]](#cite_note-27)

#### 2022

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: 2022")]

Prior to the 2022 season, the Athletics traded several key players away or let them leave during free agency. These players included Olson, Chapman, Bassitt, Manaea, Canha, and Starling Marte. Because the team's future in Oakland began looking more uncertain, some observers suspected that the organization was tanking with the hopes of fielding a competitive team in a new city.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

The Athletics wound up having a disastrous 2022 season in which the team finished last in the AL West with a 60–102 record. It was the worst record in the American League and Oakland's worst record since 1979.

### Relocation to West Sacramento and Las Vegas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Relocation to West Sacramento and Las Vegas")]

Main article: [Oakland Athletics relocation to Las Vegas](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics_relocation_to_Las_Vegas "Oakland Athletics relocation to Las Vegas")

#### 2023

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: 2023")]

In April 2023, the Athletics finalized plans to [relocate](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics_relocation_to_Las_Vegas "Oakland Athletics relocation to Las Vegas") to [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas"), purchasing a 49-acre plot on the site of the [Wild Wild West Gambling Hall & Hotel](/wiki/Wild_Wild_West_Gambling_Hall_%26_Hotel "Wild Wild West Gambling Hall & Hotel") near the [Las Vegas Strip](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Strip "Las Vegas Strip") for the construction of a new ballpark, ending negotiations with the city of Oakland.[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30) On May 9, 2023, the Athletics switched their planned location to the site of [Tropicana Las Vegas](/wiki/Tropicana_Las_Vegas "Tropicana Las Vegas"), which was demolished in October to make room for [a 33,000-seat retractable roof stadium](/wiki/New_Las_Vegas_Stadium "New Las Vegas Stadium").[[30]](#cite_note-31) By June 2023, the team's 33,000-seat ballpark was approved through the Nevada Legislature voting in favor of its bill SB1 and sent to the desk of Governor [Joe Lombardo](/wiki/Joe_Lombardo "Joe Lombardo") where he would sign it into law. After SB1's signing, the Athletics announced the relocation process to the Las Vegas area would begin with the team drafting an application for the move by June 21.[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34) The team would submit its relocation application fully on August 21.[[34]](#cite_note-Fisher082323-35) The Athletics finished the 2023 season with a 50–112 record, the worst in the major leagues.

#### 2024

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: 2024")]

On November 16, 2023, the Athletics received official approval from MLB to relocate to Las Vegas.[[35]](#cite_note-36) By April, the team officially announced that 2024 would be its final season in Oakland and would spend three seasons at [West Sacramento](/wiki/West_Sacramento "West Sacramento")'s [Sutter Health Park](/wiki/Sutter_Health_Park "Sutter Health Park") until their new ballpark in Las Vegas is complete.[[36]](#cite_note-37) The Athletics played their final game in Oakland on September 26, 2024, winning 3–2 against the [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)") in front of 46,889 fans. The Athletics finished 69–93 that season, fourth in the AL West. On September 29, 2024, the Athletics played their final game as an Oakland-based team, losing 1–2 against the [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/Seattle_Mariners "Seattle Mariners") on the road.

On May 13, 2024, in a game between the [Houston Astros](/wiki/Houston_Astros "Houston Astros") and the Athletics, [Jenny Cavnar](/wiki/Jenny_Cavnar "Jenny Cavnar") and [Julia Morales](/wiki/Julia_Morales "Julia Morales") became the first two women to do the play-by-play on television for the same [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") game.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

#### Fan reaction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Fan reaction")]

The A's plan to relocate to Las Vegas has garnered an overwhelmingly negative reception from Bay Area fans, baseball writers, former executives, and even some current players, including Las Vegas natives [Bryce Harper](/wiki/Bryce_Harper "Bryce Harper"), [Bryson Stott](/wiki/Bryson_Stott "Bryson Stott"), and [Paul Sewald](/wiki/Paul_Sewald "Paul Sewald").[[b]](#cite_note-47) Many have argued that Fisher's lack of spending on the team was a deliberate effort to sink the club and keep fans away from the Coliseum in order to sabotage negotiations in Oakland.[[46]](#cite_note-48)[[47]](#cite_note-49) Others have opined that by many measures, such as public money available and market size, Oakland was actually offering the better stadium deal, and that the relocation was purely an effort for the A's to remain on revenue sharing with no other factors considered, as some commentators have speculated that Fisher was no longer able to afford his part of the Howard Terminal project.[[38]](#cite_note-espn.com-39)[[48]](#cite_note-50) Outside of Oakland, fan protests against the move took place at [Oracle Park](/wiki/Oracle_Park "Oracle Park") in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), [Coors Field](/wiki/Coors_Field "Coors Field") in [Denver](/wiki/Denver "Denver"), [Nationals Park](/wiki/Nationals_Park "Nationals Park") in [Washington D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), and the [2023 MLB All-Star Game](/wiki/2023_Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "2023 Major League Baseball All-Star Game") at [T-Mobile Park](/wiki/T-Mobile_Park "T-Mobile Park") in [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle").[[49]](#cite_note-51)[[50]](#cite_note-52)[[51]](#cite_note-53)[[52]](#cite_note-54) Many of Manfred's comments and actions during the process received backlash as well, with several commentators feeling that he was being disrespectful towards Oakland and ignoring the reality of the situation in order to support an owner who could not afford to keep the team in Oakland.[[53]](#cite_note-55)[[54]](#cite_note-56)[[55]](#cite_note-57) The Sell the Team movement continued into the 2024 season, and some fans have also begun supporting the [Oakland Ballers](/wiki/Oakland_Ballers "Oakland Ballers") of the [Pioneer League](/wiki/Pioneer_League_(baseball) "Pioneer League (baseball)").

## Uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Uniforms")]

At the start of their tenure in Oakland, owner [Charles O. Finley](/wiki/Charles_O._Finley "Charles O. Finley") used the team's colors of what he termed "Kelly Green, Wedding Gown White and Fort Knox Gold", which was changed during the team's tenure in [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City_Athletics "Kansas City Athletics"). It was here that he began experimenting with dramatic uniforms to match these bright colors, such as gold sleeveless tops with green undershirts and gold pants. The uniform innovations increased after the team's move to Oakland, which came with the introduction of polyester pullover uniforms.

During their dynasty years in the 1970s, the A's had dozens of uniform combinations with jerseys and pants in all three team colors, and never wore the traditional gray on the road, instead wearing green or gold, which helped to contribute to their nickname of "The Swingin' A's". After the team's sale to the [Haas](/wiki/Walter_A._Haas_Jr. "Walter A. Haas Jr.") family, the team changed its primary color to a more subdued forest green in 1982 and began a move back to more traditional uniforms.

The 2023 team wore home uniforms with "Athletics" spelled out in script writing and road uniforms with "Oakland" spelled out in script writing, with the cap logo consisting of the traditional "A" with "apostrophe-s". The home cap, which was also the team's road cap until 1992, is forest green with a gold bill and white lettering. This design was also the basis of their batting helmet, which is used both at home and on the road. The road cap, which initially debuted in 1993, is all-forest green. The first version had the white "A's" wordmark before it was changed to gold the following season. An all-forest green batting helmet was paired with this cap until 2008. In 2014, the "A's" wordmark returned to white but added gold trim.

From 1994 until 2013, the A's wore green [alternate jerseys](/wiki/Third_jersey "Third jersey") with the word "Athletics" in gold, for both road and home games.

During the 2000s, the Athletics introduced black as one of their colors. They began wearing a black alternate jersey with "Athletics" written in green. After a brief discontinuance, the A's brought back the black jersey, this time with "Athletics" written in white with gold highlights. The cap paired with this jersey is all-black, initially with the green and white-trimmed "A's" wordmark, before switching to a white and gold-trimmed "A's" wordmark. Commercially popular but rarely chosen as the alternate by players, the black uniform was retired in 2011 in favor of a gold alternate jersey.

The gold alternate has "A's" in green trimmed in white on the left chest. With the exception of several road games during the 2011 season, the Athletics' gold uniforms were used as the designated home alternates. A green version of their gold alternates was introduced for the 2014 season, serving as a replacement to the previous green alternates. The new green alternates featured the piping, "A's" and lettering in white with gold trim.

In 2018, as part of the franchise's 50th anniversary since the move to Oakland, the A's wore a kelly green alternate uniform with "Oakland" in white with gold trim, and was paired with an all-kelly green cap.[[56]](#cite_note-58) This set was later worn with an alternate kelly green helmet with gold visor. This uniform eventually supplanted the gold alternates by 2019, and in 2022, after the forest green alternate was retired, it became the team's only active alternate uniform.

The nickname "A's" has long been used interchangeably with "Athletics", dating to the team's early days when headline writers used it to shorten the name. From 1972 through 1980, the team name was officially "Oakland A's", although the [Commissioner's Trophy](/wiki/Commissioner%27s_Trophy_(MLB) "Commissioner's Trophy (MLB)"), given out annually to the winner of baseball's [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series"), still listed the team's name as the "Oakland Athletics" on the gold-plated pennant representing the Oakland franchise. According to Bill Libby's Book, *Charlie O and the Angry A's*, owner Charlie O. Finley banned the word "Athletics" from the club's name because he felt that name was too closely associated with former Philadelphia Athletics owner Connie Mack, and he wanted the name "Oakland A's" to become just as closely associated with him. The name also vaguely suggested the name of the old minor league [Oakland Oaks](/wiki/Oakland_Oaks_(PCL) "Oakland Oaks (PCL)"), which were alternatively called the "Acorns". New owner Walter Haas restored the official name to "Athletics" in 1981, but retained the nickname "A's" for marketing. At first, the word "Athletics" was restored only to the club's logo, underneath the much larger stylized-"A" that had come to represent the team since the early days. By 1987, however, the word returned, in script lettering, to the front of the team's jerseys.

Prior to the mid-2010s, the A's had a long-standing tradition of wearing white cleats team-wide (in line with the standard MLB practice that required all uniformed team members to wear a base cleat color), which dated to the Finley ownership. Since the mid-2010s, however, MLB has gradually relaxed its shoe color rules, and several A's players began wearing cleats in non-white colors, such as [Jed Lowrie](/wiki/Jed_Lowrie "Jed Lowrie")'s green cleats.
