## Ballpark history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Ballpark history")]

The [Oakland Coliseum](/wiki/Oakland_Coliseum "Oakland Coliseum")—originally the Oakland–Alameda County Coliseum, and later named as Network Associates, [McAfee](/wiki/McAfee "McAfee"), [Overstock.com](/wiki/Overstock.com "Overstock.com")/O.co and [RingCentral](/wiki/RingCentral "RingCentral") Coliseum—was built as a multi-purpose facility. [Louisiana Superdome](/wiki/Louisiana_Superdome "Louisiana Superdome") officials pursued negotiations with Oakland Athletics officials during the 1978–79 baseball offseason about moving the Oakland Athletics to their facility in [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"). The Oakland Athletics were unable to break their lease at the Coliseum, and remained in Oakland.[[57]](#cite_note-59)

After the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Raiders "Las Vegas Raiders") moved to Los Angeles in 1982, many improvements were made to what was suddenly a baseball-only facility. The 1994 movie *[Angels in the Outfield](/wiki/Angels_in_the_Outfield_(1994_film) "Angels in the Outfield (1994 film)")* was filmed in part at the Coliseum, filling in for [Anaheim Stadium](/wiki/Angel_Stadium "Angel Stadium") following the damage from the [1994 Northridge earthquake](/wiki/1994_Northridge_earthquake "1994 Northridge earthquake").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/1984_Mother%27s_Cookies_-_Oakland_Coliseum.JPG/250px-1984_Mother%27s_Cookies_-_Oakland_Coliseum.JPG)](/wiki/File:1984_Mother%27s_Cookies_-_Oakland_Coliseum.JPG)

The Coliseum in its original configuration before being enclosed

In 1995, the Raiders moved back to Oakland. The Coliseum was expanded to 63,026 seats. The bucolic view of the Oakland foothills that baseball spectators enjoyed was replaced with a jarring view of an outfield grandstand contemptuously referred to as "[Mount Davis](/wiki/Mount_Davis_(Oakland) "Mount Davis (Oakland)")" after Raiders' owner [Al Davis](/wiki/Al_Davis "Al Davis"). Because construction was not finished by the start of the [1996](/wiki/1996_Major_League_Baseball_season "1996 Major League Baseball season") season, the Athletics were forced to play their first six-game homestand at 9,300-seat [Cashman Field](/wiki/Cashman_Field "Cashman Field") in [Las Vegas, Nevada](/wiki/Las_Vegas,_Nevada "Las Vegas, Nevada").[[58]](#cite_note-60)

Although official capacity was listed as 43,662 for baseball, seats were sometimes sold in Mount Davis, pushing actual capacity to nearly 60,000. The ready availability of tickets on game day made season tickets a tough sell, while crowds as high as 30,000 often seemed sparse in such a venue. On December 21, 2005, the Athletics announced that seats in the Coliseum's third deck would not be sold for the 2006 season, but would instead be covered with a tarp, and that tickets would no longer be sold in Mount Davis under any circumstances. That effectively reduced capacity to 34,077, making the Coliseum the lowest-capacity stadium in Major League Baseball. Beginning in 2008, sections 316–318 immediately behind home plate were the only third-deck sections open for A's games, which brought the total capacity to 35,067 until 2017, when new team president [Dave Kaval](/wiki/Dave_Kaval "Dave Kaval") took the tarps off of the upper deck, increasing capacity to 47,170. The Athletics were the last MLB team to share a stadium full-time with an NFL team, a situation that ended when the Raiders [moved](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders_relocation_to_Las_Vegas "Oakland Raiders relocation to Las Vegas") to Las Vegas in 2020.

The Oakland Athletics' spring training facility was [Hohokam Stadium](/wiki/Hohokam_Stadium "Hohokam Stadium"), in [Mesa, Arizona](/wiki/Mesa,_Arizona "Mesa, Arizona"). From 1982 to 2014, their spring training facility was [Phoenix Municipal Stadium](/wiki/Phoenix_Municipal_Stadium "Phoenix Municipal Stadium"), in [Phoenix, Arizona](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"); they also spent time playing in [Scottsdale, Arizona](/wiki/Scottsdale,_Arizona "Scottsdale, Arizona").[[59]](#cite_note-61)[[60]](#cite_note-62)

### Improvements to the Coliseum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Improvements to the Coliseum")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Oakland_Coliseum_aerial_view_2024.jpg/250px-Oakland_Coliseum_aerial_view_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oakland_Coliseum_aerial_view_2024.jpg)

[Oakland Coliseum](/wiki/Oakland_Coliseum "Oakland Coliseum"), which was the 5th oldest MLB stadium by the time of the A's last game.

#### New areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: New areas")]

In 2017, the team created an outdoor plaza in the space between the Coliseum and Oracle Arena. The grassy area is open to all ticketed fans, and it features food trucks, seating and games like [corn hole](/wiki/Corn_hole "Corn hole") for every Athletics home game.[[61]](#cite_note-63)[[62]](#cite_note-64) The following year, the team introduced The Treehouse, a 10,000-square-foot (930 m2) area open to all fans with two full-service bars, standing-room and lounge seating, numerous televisions with pre-game and postgame entertainment. The A's Stomping Ground transformed part of the Eastside Club and the area near the right-field flag poles into a fun and interactive space for kids and families. The inside section features a stage and video wall for interactive events, a digital experience that lets youngsters race their favorite Athletics players, replica team dugouts, a simulated hitting and pitching machine, foosball, and a photo booth. The outside area includes play areas, a grassy seating area, drink rails for parents, and picnic tables, a miniature baseball field and spiderweb play area.[[63]](#cite_note-65)

#### Premium spaces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Premium spaces")]

The team added three new premium spaces, including The Terrace, Lounge Seats, and the Coppola Theater Boxes, to the Coliseum for the 2019 season. The new premium seating options offer fans a high-end game-day experience with luxury amenities. The team also added two new group spaces – the Budweiser Hero Deck and Golden Road Landing – to the Coliseum.[[64]](#cite_note-66)

#### Other additions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Other additions")]

In addition, the tarps on the upper deck were removed; a modern version of the beloved mechanical Harvey the Rabbit to deliver the first pitch ball was re-introduced, while the playing surface at the Coliseum was renamed "Rickey Henderson Field". The team held the first free game in MLB history for 46,028 fans on April 17, 2018, to celebrate the 50th anniversary of the Athletics first game in Oakland.[[65]](#cite_note-67) The team tried a new concept in season ticketing in the A's Access plan that involved "general admission access to every home game with a set number of reserved-seat upgrades allotted", which was meant to replace previous attempts at subscription-based services that they tried with Ballpark Pass and Treehouse Pass.[[66]](#cite_note-hallballpark-68) On July 21, 2018, the Athletics set a Coliseum record for the largest attendance with a crowd of 56,310 when the team hosted to the San Francisco Giants.[[66]](#cite_note-hallballpark-68)[[67]](#cite_note-69)

### Prior stadium proposals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Prior stadium proposals")]

#### Oakland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Oakland")]

Main article: [Oakland Ballpark](/wiki/Oakland_Ballpark "Oakland Ballpark")

Since the early 2000s, the Oakland A's had been in talks with Oakland and other [Northern California](/wiki/Northern_California "Northern California") cities about building a new baseball-only stadium. The team had said it wanted to remain in Oakland. A 2017 plan would have placed a new 35,000 seat A's stadium near [Laney College](/wiki/Laney_College "Laney College") and the Eastlake neighborhood on the site of the Peralta Community College District's administration buildings. The plan was announced by team president [Dave Kaval](/wiki/Dave_Kaval "Dave Kaval") in September 2017.[[68]](#cite_note-70) However, three months later, negotiations abruptly ended.[[69]](#cite_note-71) On November 28, 2018, the Oakland Athletics announced that the team had chosen to build its new 34,000-seat ballpark at the Howard Terminal site at the [Port of Oakland](/wiki/Port_of_Oakland "Port of Oakland"). The team also announced its intent to purchase the Coliseum site and renovate it into a tech and housing hub, preserving [Oakland Arena](/wiki/Oakland_Arena "Oakland Arena") and reducing the Coliseum to a low-rise sports park as San Francisco did with [Kezar Stadium](/wiki/Kezar_Stadium "Kezar Stadium").[[70]](#cite_note-72) In April 2023, the City of Oakland ended discussions with the Oakland Athletics organization after the announcement of a new ballpark in Las Vegas, amid widespread claims that the team was not negotiating in good faith and was using the proposed site in Oakland to leverage a better deal in Las Vegas instead of any real intention to stay within the city.[[71]](#cite_note-73)

#### Fremont

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Fremont")]

On November 7, 2006, the news media announced the Athletics would be leaving Oakland as early as 2010 for a new stadium in Fremont, confirmed the next day by the Fremont City Council. The plan was strongly supported by Fremont Mayor [Bob Wasserman](/wiki/Bob_Wasserman "Bob Wasserman").[[72]](#cite_note-argus-74) The team would have played in [Cisco Field](/wiki/Cisco_Field "Cisco Field"), a 32,000-seat, baseball-only facility.[[73]](#cite_note-75) The proposed ballpark would have been part of a larger "ballpark village" which would have included retail and residential development. On February 24, 2009, however, Lew Wolff released an open letter announcing the end of his efforts to relocate the A's to Fremont, citing "real and threatened" delays to the project.[[74]](#cite_note-76) The project faced opposition from some in the community who thought the relocation of the A's to Fremont would increase traffic problems in the city and decrease property values near the ballpark site.

#### San Jose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: San Jose")]

In 2009, the City of [San Jose](/wiki/San_Jose,_California "San Jose, California") attempted to open negotiations with the team regarding a move to the city. Although land south of [Diridon Station](/wiki/Diridon_Station "Diridon Station") would be acquired by the city as a stadium site, the [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/San_Francisco_Giants "San Francisco Giants")' claim on [Santa Clara County](/wiki/Santa_Clara_County "Santa Clara County") as part of their home territory would have to be settled before any agreement could be made.[[75]](#cite_note-77)

By 2010, San Jose was "aggressively wooing" Oakland A's owner [Lew Wolff](/wiki/Lew_Wolff "Lew Wolff"), the city as the team's "best option", but Major League Baseball Commissioner [Bud Selig](/wiki/Bud_Selig "Bud Selig") said he would await a report on whether the team could move to the area, because of the Giants conflict.[[76]](#cite_note-78) In September 2010, 75 [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley") CEOs drafted and signed a letter to Bud Selig urging a timely approval of the move to San Jose.[[77]](#cite_note-79) In May 2011, San Jose Mayor [Chuck Reed](/wiki/Chuck_Reed "Chuck Reed") sent a letter to Bud Selig asking the commissioner for a timetable of when he might decide whether the A's can pursue this new ballpark, but Selig did not respond.[[78]](#cite_note-80)

Selig addressed the San Jose issue via an online town hall forum held in July 2011, saying, "Well, the latest is, I have a small committee who has really assessed that whole situation, Oakland, San Francisco, and it is complex. You talk about complex situations; they have done a terrific job. I know there are some people who think it's taken too long and I understand that. I'm willing to accept that. But you make decisions like this; I've always said, you'd better be careful. Better to get it done right than to get it done fast. But we'll make a decision that's based on logic and reason at the proper time."[[79]](#cite_note-81)

On June 18, 2013, the City of San Jose filed suit against Selig, seeking the court's ruling that Major League Baseball may not prevent the Oakland A's from moving to San Jose.[[80]](#cite_note-82) Wolff criticized the lawsuit, stating he did not believe business disputes should be settled through legal action.[[81]](#cite_note-83)

Most of the city's claims were dismissed in October 2013, but a U.S. District Judge ruled that San Jose could move forward with its claim that MLB illegally interfered with a land agreement between the city and the A's. On January 15, 2015, a three-judge panel of the [9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals](/wiki/9th_U.S._Circuit_Court_of_Appeals "9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals") ruled unanimously that the claims were barred by baseball's antitrust exemption, established by the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court") in 1922 and upheld in 1953 and 1972. San Jose Mayor Sam Liccardo commented that the city would seek a ruling from the U.S. Supreme Court.[[82]](#cite_note-84) On October 5, 2015, the United States Supreme Court rejected San Jose's case.[[83]](#cite_note-85)

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

### San Francisco Giants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: San Francisco Giants")]

See also: [Bay Bridge Series](/wiki/Bay_Bridge_Series "Bay Bridge Series")

The Bay Bridge Series was the name of a series of games played between (and the rivalry of) the Oakland A's and [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/San_Francisco_Giants "San Francisco Giants") of the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)"). The series took its name from the [San Francisco–Oakland Bay Bridge](/wiki/San_Francisco%E2%80%93Oakland_Bay_Bridge "San Francisco–Oakland Bay Bridge") which links the cities of [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland,_California "Oakland, California") and San Francisco. Although competitive, the regional rivalry between the A's and Giants was considered a friendly one with mostly mutual companionship between the fans, as opposed to [White Sox–Cubs](/wiki/White_Sox_%E2%80%93_Cubs_rivalry "White Sox – Cubs rivalry"), or [Yankees–Mets](/wiki/Mets%E2%80%93Yankees_rivalry "Mets–Yankees rivalry") games where animosity ran high. Hats displaying both teams on the cap were sold from vendors at the games, and once in a while the teams both dressed in original team uniforms from the early era of baseball.
The series was also occasionally referred to as the "BART Series" for the [Bay Area Rapid Transit](/wiki/Bay_Area_Rapid_Transit "Bay Area Rapid Transit") system that links Oakland to San Francisco. However, the name "BART Series" had never been popular beyond a small selection of history books and national broadcasters and had fallen out of favor. Bay Area locals almost exclusively referred to the rivalry as the "Battle of the Bay".[[84]](#cite_note-Bolavip-86)

Originally, the term described a series of exhibition games played between the two clubs after the conclusion of spring training, immediately prior to the start of the regular season. It was first used to refer to the [1989 World Series](/wiki/1989_World_Series "1989 World Series") in which the Athletics won their most recent championship and the first time the teams had met since they moved to the [San Francisco Bay Area](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Area "San Francisco Bay Area") (and the first time they had met since the A's also defeated the Giants in the [1913 World Series](/wiki/1913_World_Series "1913 World Series")). Since the commencement of [interleague play](/wiki/Interleague_play "Interleague play"), it also referred to games played between the teams during the regular season since 1997. In its existence, the Athletics had won 76 regular season games, and the Giants had won 72 contests.[[85]](#cite_note-87)

The A's also had edges on the Giants in terms of overall postseason appearances (21–13), division titles (17–10) and World Series titles (4–3) since both teams moved to the Bay Area, even though the Giants franchise moved there a decade earlier than the A's did.

On March 24, 2018, the Oakland A's announced that for the Sunday, March 25, 2018, exhibition game against the San Francisco Giants, A's fans would be charged $30 for parking and Giants fans would be charged $50. However, the A's stated that Giants fans could receive $20 off if they shout "Go A's" at the parking gates.[[86]](#cite_note-88)

In 2018, the Athletics and Giants started battling for a "Bay Bridge" Trophy[[87]](#cite_note-89) made from steel taken from the [old east span](/wiki/Eastern_span_replacement_of_the_San_Francisco%E2%80%93Oakland_Bay_Bridge "Eastern span replacement of the San Francisco–Oakland Bay Bridge") of the Bay Bridge, which was taken down after the new span was opened in 2013.[[88]](#cite_note-90)[[89]](#cite_note-91) The A's won the inaugural season with the trophy, allowing them to place their logo atop its Bay Bridge stand.[[90]](#cite_note-92) When the A's left Oakland, the Giants had won the trophy 4 times, to the A's 3.

### Los Angeles Angels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Los Angeles Angels")]

See also: [Angels–Athletics rivalry](/wiki/Angels%E2%80%93Athletics_rivalry "Angels–Athletics rivalry")

The A's had held a rivalry with the [Los Angeles Angels](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Angels "Los Angeles Angels") since their relocation to California in 1968, and the charter membership of both teams in the AL West in 1969. The A's and Angels had often competed for the division title.[[91]](#cite_note-93) The peak of the rivalry was during the early part of the millennium as both teams were perennial contenders. During the [2002](/wiki/2002_Major_League_Baseball_season "2002 Major League Baseball season") season, the A's famous "Moneyball" tactics led them to a league record 20-game winning streak, knocking the Angels out of the first seed in the division. The A's finished 4 games ahead while the Angels secured the Wild Card berth.[[92]](#cite_note-94) Despite the 103-win season for Oakland, they lost to the underdog Minnesota Twins in the [ALDS](/wiki/2002_American_League_Division_Series "2002 American League Division Series"). The Angels beat the heavily favored New York Yankees, then beat the Twins, and then won the [2002 World Series](/wiki/2002_World_Series "2002 World Series"). During the [2004](/wiki/2004_Major_League_Baseball_season "2004 Major League Baseball season") season, the teams were tied for wins headed into the final week of September with the last three games being played in Oakland against the Angels.[[93]](#cite_note-95) Both teams were battling to secure the division championship. Oakland lost two of the three games to the Angels, and they were eliminated from the playoff hunt. The Angels were swept in the playoffs by the eventual champion Boston Red Sox.[[94]](#cite_note-96) While in Oakland, the Athletics lead the series 492–414. The two teams never met in the postseason.

### Retired numbers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Retired numbers")]

See also: [List of Major League Baseball retired numbers](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_retired_numbers "List of Major League Baseball retired numbers")

The Oakland Athletics had retired six numbers; additionally, [Walter A. Haas, Jr.](/wiki/Walter_A._Haas,_Jr. "Walter A. Haas, Jr."), owner of the team from 1980 until his death in 1995, was honored by the retirement of the letter "A". Of the six players with retired numbers, five were retired for their play with the Oakland Athletics and one, 42, was universally retired by Major League Baseball when they honored the 50th anniversary of Jackie Robinson's breaking the color barrier. No A's player from the Philadelphia era had his number retired by the organization. Though Jackson and Hunter played small portions of their careers in Kansas City, no player that played the majority of his years in the Kansas City era had his number retired either. The Oakland A's had retired only the numbers of Hall-of-Famers who played large portions of their careers in Oakland. The Oakland Athletics had all of the numbers of the Hall-of-Fame players from the Philadelphia Athletics displayed at their stadium, as well as all of the years that the Philadelphia Athletics won World Championships (1910, 1911, 1913, 1929, and 1930). [Dave Stewart](/wiki/Dave_Stewart_(baseball) "Dave Stewart (baseball)") was about to have his #34 jersey retired by the Oakland Athletics in 2020, but the ceremony was postponed until further notice, due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"). Questions were raised if there would be a formal ceremony after no news about a reschedule happened in 2021 before it was announced in April 2022 that Stewart would have his jersey retired on September 11, 2022.[[95]](#cite_note-97)[[96]](#cite_note-98) Stewart broke the A's tradition in that his number was a re-retirement, as well as his not being in the Hall of Fame.

### Oakland Athletics Hall of Fame

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Oakland Athletics Hall of Fame")]

On August 14, 2018, the team publicly announced the creation of a team Hall of Fame, complete with the first seven names to be inducted.[[97]](#cite_note-99) On September 5, the Oakland Athletics held a ceremony to induct seven members into the inaugural class. Each member was honored with an unveiling of a painting in their likeness and a bright green jacket. Hunter, who died in 1999, was represented by his widow, while Finley, who died in 1996, was represented by his son. Had the Oakland Athletics got a new stadium in Oakland, a physical site would have been designated for the Hall of Fame, as the Coliseum does not have enough space for a full-fledged exhibit.[[98]](#cite_note-100) In August 2021, it was announced that players [Sal Bando](/wiki/Sal_Bando "Sal Bando"), [Eric Chavez](/wiki/Eric_Chavez "Eric Chavez"), [Joe Rudi](/wiki/Joe_Rudi "Joe Rudi"), director of player development Keith Lieppman, and clubhouse manager Steve "Vuc" Vucinich would be part of the class of 2022; in November 2021, [Ray Fosse](/wiki/Ray_Fosse "Ray Fosse"), who had died the previous month, was posthumously inducted into the Hall of Fame.[[99]](#cite_note-101)[[100]](#cite_note-102) The 2023 & 2024 classes were inducted in August of each respective year.[[101]](#cite_note-103)[[102]](#cite_note-104)

## Radio and television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Radio and television")]

See also: [List of Athletics broadcasters](/wiki/List_of_Athletics_broadcasters "List of Athletics broadcasters")

As of the 2020 season, the Oakland Athletics had 14 radio homes.[[103]](#cite_note-105) The Oakland Athletics' [flagship](/wiki/Flagship_station "Flagship station") radio station was [KNEW](/wiki/KNEW_(AM) "KNEW (AM)") and the team had a free live 24/7 exclusive A's station branded as *A's Cast* to stream the radio broadcast within the Oakland Athletics market and other A's programming via [iHeartRadio](/wiki/IHeartRadio "IHeartRadio").[[104]](#cite_note-106) Going into the 2020 season, the Oakland Athletics had a deal with [TuneIn](/wiki/TuneIn "TuneIn") for A's Cast and no flagship radio station in the Bay Area but changed their plans due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") keeping fans from attending games.[[105]](#cite_note-107) The announcing team featured [Ken Korach](/wiki/Ken_Korach "Ken Korach") and [Vince Cotroneo](/wiki/Vince_Cotroneo "Vince Cotroneo").

Television coverage was exclusively on [NBC Sports California](/wiki/NBC_Sports_California "NBC Sports California"). Some A's games aired on an alternate feed of NBCS, called NBCS Plus, if the main channel showed a [Sacramento Kings](/wiki/Sacramento_Kings "Sacramento Kings") or [San Jose Sharks](/wiki/San_Jose_Sharks "San Jose Sharks") game at the same time. On TV, [Jenny Cavnar](/wiki/Jenny_Cavnar "Jenny Cavnar") covered [play-by-play](/wiki/Play-by-play "Play-by-play"), and [Dallas Braden](/wiki/Dallas_Braden "Dallas Braden") provided [color commentary](/wiki/Color_commentary "Color commentary"). Some games would feature [Chris Caray](/wiki/Chris_Caray "Chris Caray") on play-by-play; Caray is a fourth-generation baseball announcer that included great-grandfather [Harry Caray](/wiki/Harry_Caray "Harry Caray"), grandfather [Skip Caray](/wiki/Skip_Caray "Skip Caray"), and father [Chip Caray](/wiki/Chip_Caray "Chip Caray").

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oakland_Athletics&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The 2003 [Michael Lewis](/wiki/Michael_Lewis "Michael Lewis") book *[Moneyball](/wiki/Moneyball:_The_Art_of_Winning_an_Unfair_Game "Moneyball: The Art of Winning an Unfair Game")* chronicles the [2002 Oakland Athletics season](/wiki/2002_Oakland_Athletics_season "2002 Oakland Athletics season"), with a focus on [Billy Beane](/wiki/Billy_Beane "Billy Beane")'s economic approach to managing the organization under significant financial constraints. Beginning in June 2003, the book remained on [*The New York Times* Best Seller list](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Best_Seller_list "The New York Times Best Seller list") for 18 consecutive weeks, peaking at number 2.[[106]](#cite_note-108)[[107]](#cite_note-109) In 2011, [Columbia Pictures](/wiki/Columbia_Pictures "Columbia Pictures") released a [film adaptation](/wiki/Moneyball_(film) "Moneyball (film)") based on Lewis' book, which featured [Brad Pitt](/wiki/Brad_Pitt "Brad Pitt") playing the role of Beane. On September 19, 2011, the U.S. premiere of *Moneyball* was held at the [Paramount Theatre](/wiki/Paramount_Theatre_(Oakland,_California) "Paramount Theatre (Oakland, California)") in Oakland, which featured a green carpet for attendees to walk, rather than the traditional [red carpet](/wiki/Red_carpet "Red carpet").[[108]](#cite_note-110)

The blog that spawned the full-fledged popular sports blog site *[SBNation](/wiki/SBNation "SBNation")* was dedicated to the Oakland Athletics.[[109]](#cite_note-111)[[110]](#cite_note-112)

[Eric Shaun Lynch](/wiki/Eric_Shaun_Lynch "Eric Shaun Lynch"), a former member of *[The Howard Stern Show](/wiki/The_Howard_Stern_Show "The Howard Stern Show")'*s [Wack Pack](/wiki/Wack_Pack "Wack Pack") who went by the name "Eric the Actor" (and previously, "Eric the Midget"), was a huge fan of the Oakland Athletics and would occasionally talk about them on Stern's show. Following his death in September 2014, the team broadcasters offered a tribute by using Lynch's signature sign off "bye for now" at the end of an Oakland Athletics game broadcast. During the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic, when American baseball teams were using cutouts of fans to show solidarity in their absence, the Oakland Athletics placed a cutout of Lynch among other cutouts of the team's fans.

The Oakland A's are mentioned in the song "Prize Fighter" by American rock band [the Killers](/wiki/The_Killers "The Killers"), from the deluxe edition of their fourth studio album, [*Battle Born*](/wiki/Battle_Born_(album) "Battle Born (album)") (2012). At the start of the song, the lyric "and I heard she used to throw, for The Oakland A's" can be heard.[[111]](#cite_note-113)
